content.full_text:
CITY OF SOUTH SAN FRANCISCO
CALIFORNIA

OFFICE OF THE
CITY ATTORNEY

July 27, 1990

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA. 95804

Re: Request for Written Advice (Government Code Section 83114(b))

Dear Ms. Donovan:

As the City Attorney for the City of South San Francisco on behalf of Councilman
John Penna, I am requesting written advice pursuant to Government Code Section
83114(b) regarding the following matter.

BACKGROUND

The City of South San Francisco is a community of approximately 52,000 persons.
There are 1,500 acres in the City limits zoned for commercial and industrial
use, out of a total of 9.53 square miles. Approximately one fourth of the City
is zoned commercial and industrial and three-fourths is zoned residential.

At the behest of a developer, the City of South San Francisco, through its
Redevelopment Agency (same members as City Council) created a redevelopment
project known as Shearwater for a site encompassing 54 acres of dry land and
133 acres of water. A master planned mixed use development is proposed.

At present, the City owns a parcel of property, approximately six acres in
size, considered to be integral to the development. Pursuant to an outstanding
agreement with the developer, the City anticipates selling or leasing this parcel
to the developer. Details of the transaction are being negotiated by the City
Manager and the developer, subject to direction and ultimate approval by the
City Council. It is expected that the transaction will be concluded in the
fall of 1990.

The individual components of the project, such as the approval of the final
subdivision map and all of the approvals for the buildings (retail, office,
hotel, possible condominiums) as well as the specific design of a marina,
will be subject to various discretionary approval processes as the project is
constructed in phases. In addition, the developer has requested the city
establish an assessment district and a landscape and lighting maintenance
district for various financing purposes. The formation of these districts
is expected to occur in September.

City Hall Annex          315 Maple Avenue 94080          (415) 877-8515
                         P.O. Box 711 94083

Kathryn E. Donovan
July 27, 1990
Page 2

In November 1989 John Penna was elected to the City Council. Mr. Penna is a
broker and owns Penna Realty, a local real estate company for the past 26 years.
Over 80% of Mr. Penna's business is done in South San Francisco. The business
includes property management, real estate sales of residential, commercial and
industrial property, and real estate appraising and consulting.

In December 1989 and January 1990, the Shearwater project began to move forward
again after a hiatus and Councilman Penna participated in some discussions and
fairly routine development actions, and received briefings on ownership structure
and an appraisal report pertaining to the City's parcel.

During March 1990 Councilman Penna approached the Shearwater developer, who he
has known and dealt with since 1984, although no deals were ever completed, and
offered to present the Shearwater Project at an upcoming International Association
of Realtors [FIABCI] conference in Acapulco, Mexico. Pursuant to an authorizing
letter, obtained by Councilman Penna, on behalf of FIABCI,
he made a presentation on the Shearwater Project to approximately 1,000 brokers
attending the conference, using materials supplied by the developer. If a broker
comes forward as a result of that presentation and goes through Mr. Penna to
present an offer, Mr. Penna will receive a commission. To date, no broker has
come forward in this manner.

TRI Realtors is the agent for marketing and leasing the Shearwater site. Any
broker, however, can bring a client directly to the developer and receive a full
commission for the leasing or sale of space in the project. Prior to the
conference, Councilman Penna worked with a broker who had clients potentially
interested in acquiring the hotel site and building shell for a sum in the range
of $135 million. To date, no sales transaction has been consummated. Councilman
Penna indicates that to his knowledge none is forthcoming.

In May I advised Councilman Penna that due to the then current negotiations for
the hotel and the pending presentation in Mexico that he should refrain from
participating in several Shearwater matters coming before the Agency/Council,
and he followed that advice. Since the approvals being sought relate to the
financial feasibility of the project and since the discussion concerning the
terms of sale of the City-owned property have a financial impact on the project,
to the extent Councilman Penna was pursuing a business relationship with the
developer it was my opinion, after seeking oral advice from the FPPC, that it
did not seem appropriate for him to participate in Agency/Council business.

As indicated previously, to date there have not been any completed sales
resulting in a financial return to Councilman Penna, nor are there any pending
sales according to Councilman Penna. Consequently, he believes he has a right
to participate in future Agency/Council discussions concerning various aspects
of the project, including the lease or sale of public land and the
formation/implementation of assessment and maintenance districts. He also
believes that as a realtor he has a right to participate in transactions
throughout the City. At the moment, there are no pending transactions related
to Shearwater, however Mr. Penna acknowledges that should an opportunity present
itself in the future, he may pursue it.

Kathryn E. Donovan
July 27, 1990
Page 3

QUESTIONS

1. If Mr. Penna participates now as a member of the Agency/Council in
discussions and actions concerning the Shearwater project, and six months, or
eighteen months from now as the project is underway he wishes to engage in lease or purchase of a portion of the project, is there a conflict
and when does it arise?

2. If, as a member of the Agency/Council, Mr. Penna participates in
decisions that enable the project to go forward, does that preclude him from
being involved in the project in his capacity as a realtor?

3. When a project, such as Shearwater, has numerous integrated parts and
will require multiple approvals for different phases over a period of time, is
there a conflict of interest if a councilmember actively seeks to transact
private business during the pendency of a phase of the project or the entire
project?

4. If a City Council is engaging in negotiation and sale or lease
transactions with a developer, is there a conflict of interest if a
councilmember participates in those activities and subsequently engages in
private business transactions, particularly if the councilmember has no specific
deal pending at the time of the negotiation and sale or lease?


VALERIE J. AMENTO
City Attorney

VJA/mm

sections.question:
1. If Mr. Penna participates now as a member of the Agency/Council in discussions and actions concerning the Shearwater project, and six months, or eighteen months, from now as the project is underway he has a client who wishes to engage in lease or purchase of a portion of the project, is there a conflict and when does it arise?

2. If, as a member of the Agency/Council, Mr. Penna participates in decisions that enable the project to go forward, does that preclude him from being involved in the project in his capacity as a realtor?

3. When a project, such as Shearwater, has numerous integrated parts and will require multiple approvals for different phases over a period of time, is there a conflict of interest if a councilmember actively seeks to transact private business during the pendency of a phase of the project or the entire project?

4. If a City Council is engaging in negotiation and sale or lease transactions with a developer, is there a conflict of interest if a councilmember participates in those activities and subsequently engages in private business transactions, particularly if the councilmember has no specific deal pending at the time of the negotiation and sale or lease?

VALERIE J. ARMENTO  
City Attorney  
VJA/mm
sections.facts:
The City of South San Francisco is a community of approximately 52,000 persons. There are 1,500 acres in the City limits zoned for commercial and industrial use, out of a total of 9.53 square miles. Approximately one fourth of the City is zoned commercial and industrial and three-fourths is zoned residential.

At the behest of a developer, the City of South San Francisco, through its Redevelopment Agency (same members as City Council) created a redevelopment project known as Shearwater for a site encompassing 54 acres of dry land and 133 acres of water. A master planned mixed use development is proposed.

At present, the City owns a parcel of property, approximately six acres in size, considered to be integral to the development. Pursuant to an outstanding agreement with the developer, the City anticipates selling or leasing this parcel to the developer. Details of the transaction are being negotiated by the City Manager and the developer, subject to direction and ultimate approval by the City Council. It is expected that the transaction will be concluded in the fall of 1990.

The individual components of the project, such as the approval of the final subdivision map and all of the approvals for the buildings (retail, office, hotel, possible condominiums) as well as the specific design of a marina, will be subject to various discretionary approval processes as the project is constructed in phases. In addition, the developer has requested the city establish an assessment district and a landscape and lighting maintenance district for various financing purposes. The formation of these districts is expected to occur in September.

In November 1989 John Penna was elected to the City Council. Mr. Penna is a broker and owns Penna Realty, a local real estate company for the past 26 years. Over 80% of Mr. Penna's business is done in South San Francisco. The business includes property management, real estate sales of residential, commercial and industrial property, and real estate appraising and consulting.

In December 1989 and January 1990, the Shearwater project began to move forward again after a hiatus and Councilman Penna participated in some discussions and fairly routine development actions, and received briefings on ownership structure and an appraisal report pertaining to the City's parcel.

During March 1990 Councilman Penna approached the Shearwater developer, who he has known and dealt with since 1984, although no deals were ever completed, and offered to present the Shearwater Project at an upcoming International Association of Realtors (FIABCI) conference in Acapulco, Mexico. Pursuant to an authorizing letter, obtained by Councilman Penna from Shearwater at the request of FIABCI, he made a presentation on the Shearwater Project to approximately 1,000 brokers attending the conference, using materials supplied by the developer. If a broker comes forward as a result of that presentation and goes through Mr. Penna to present an offer, Mr. Penna will receive a commission. To date, no broker has come forward in this manner.

TRI Realtors is the agent for marketing and leasing the Shearwater site. Any broker, however, can bring a client directly to the developer and receive a full commission for the leasing or sale of space in the project. Prior to the conference, Councilman Penna worked with a broker who had clients potentially interested in acquiring the hotel site and building shell for a sum in the range of $135 million. To date, no sales transaction has been consummated. Councilman Penna indicates that to his knowledge none is forthcoming.

In May I advised Councilman Penna that due to the then current negotiations for the hotel and the pending presentation in Mexico that he should refrain from participating in several Shearwater matters coming before the Agency/Council, and he followed that advice. Since the approvals being sought relate to the financial feasibility of the project and since the discussion concerning the terms of sale of the City-owned property have a financial impact on the project, to the extent Councilmember Penna was pursuing a business relationship with the developer it was my opinion, after seeking oral advice from the FPPC, that it did not seem appropriate for him to participate in Agency/Council business.

As indicated previously, to date there have not been any completed sales resulting in a financial return to Councilman Penna, nor are there any pending sales according to Councilman Penna. Consequently, he believes he has a right to participate in future Agency/Council discussions concerning various aspects of the project, including the lease or sale of public land and the formation/implementation of assessment and maintenance districts. He also believes that as a realtor he has a right to participate in transactions throughout the City. At the moment, there are no pending transactions related to Shearwater, however Mr. Penna acknowledges that should an opportunity present itself in the future, he may pursue it.

Kathryn E. Donovan  
July 27, 1990
